title: Percent change & reverse percents
body:
**Rule.** A change of $p\%$ multiplies a value by $(1 \pm p/100)$. An increase of 20% is $\times 1.2$; a decrease of 20% is $\times 0.8$. Successive percent changes **multiply** — they never add.

**Why it matters.** Most percent traps come from adding changes (e.g. "+20% then −20% returns to start"). It does not: $1.2 \times 0.8 = 0.96$, a 4% net loss.

**Example.** A price rises 10% to \$66. The original was $66 \div 1.1 = 60$, *not* $66 \times 0.9$. To reverse a percent increase, divide; don't apply the percent backward.

title: Working with ratios
body:
**Rule.** A ratio $a : b$ means quantities are $ak$ and $bk$ for some multiplier $k$. Find $k$ from the given total or one known part, then scale.

**Why it matters.** Ratios hide the actual count. Turning $a:b$ into $ak, bk$ converts a ratio problem into ordinary arithmetic.

**Example.** Boys : girls $= 3 : 5$ with 20 girls. The 5 parts equal 20, so $k = 4$ and boys $= 3 \times 4 = 12$. The total is $(3+5)k = 32$.

title: Exponent rules
body:
**Rule.** $a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}$, $\;(a^m)^n = a^{mn}$, $\;a^{-n} = 1/a^n$, $\;a^0 = 1$.

**Why it matters.** When an equation mixes bases like 2, 4, 8, 16, rewrite everything in the **same base** and equate exponents. This turns an exponential equation into a linear one.

**Example.** $2^x \cdot 4^{x+1} = 16^{x-1}$. Write $4 = 2^2,\ 16 = 2^4$: left $= 2^{3x+2}$, right $= 2^{4x-4}$. So $3x+2 = 4x-4 \Rightarrow x = 6$.

title: Rates, distance & average speed
body:
**Rule.** Distance $=$ rate $\times$ time. **Average speed** $=$ total distance $\div$ total time — never the average of the speeds.

**Why it matters.** Equal-distance trips at two speeds tempt you to average the speeds; that is wrong unless the *times* are equal.

**Example.** 120 mi at 40 mph (3 h) then 120 mi at 60 mph (2 h): 240 mi in 5 h $= 48$ mph, not 50.

title: Work-rate problems
body:
**Rule.** Convert each worker to a **rate** (job per unit time) and **add** rates that work together; subtract a drain. If A finishes in $a$ hours, A's rate is $1/a$ per hour.

**Why it matters.** Times do not add, but rates do. Adding times is the most common mistake.

**Example.** Fill rates $1/6 + 1/4$ minus drain $1/12 = \tfrac{2+3-1}{12} = \tfrac{1}{3}$ tank/hour, so the tank fills in 3 hours.

title: Number properties essentials
body:
**Rule.** Use **prime factorization**. A perfect square has every prime exponent **even**. Divisibility, GCD, and LCM all fall out of the prime factors.

**Why it matters.** "Must be" divisibility questions are really questions about minimum prime exponents.

**Example.** If $n^2$ is divisible by $72 = 2^3 \cdot 3^2$, then $n^2$ must contain $2^4$ (exponents are even), so $n$ contains $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$. Thus 12 must divide $n$.

title: Averages & weighted averages
body:
**Rule.** Average $=$ sum $\div$ count, so **sum $=$ average $\times$ count**. For groups of different sizes, weight each average by its count.

**Why it matters.** Recovering the *sum* is the key move in most mean problems, and weighting prevents the "just average the averages" error.

**Example.** A class of 10 averages 70 and a class of 30 averages 90. Overall $= \dfrac{10\cdot 70 + 30\cdot 90}{40} = \dfrac{3400}{40} = 85$ — closer to 90 because that group is larger.

title: Counting & probability basics
body:
**Rule.** Combinations $\binom{n}{k} = \dfrac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$ count unordered selections. Probability $=$ favorable $\div$ total. For "**at least one**," use the complement: $P(\text{at least one}) = 1 - P(\text{none})$.

**Why it matters.** The complement turns messy casework into one quick subtraction.

**Example.** Choose 3 from 4 men + 3 women. $P(\text{no women}) = \binom{4}{3}/\binom{7}{3} = 4/35$, so $P(\ge 1\text{ woman}) = 1 - 4/35 = 31/35$.

title: Critical Reasoning: weaken & strengthen
body:
**Rule.** First isolate the **conclusion** and the **evidence**, then attack or defend the *gap* between them. To **weaken** a causal claim, supply an **alternative cause** or break the link; to **strengthen**, rule alternatives out.

**Why it matters.** Wrong answers are usually true statements that don't touch the gap. Right answers change how well the evidence supports the conclusion.

**Example.** "Bike-share launched, congestion fell, so bikes caused it." A new subway line opening at the same time *weakens* it (another cause); evidence that no other change occurred *strengthens* it.

title: Critical Reasoning: the negation test
body:
**Rule.** A necessary assumption is something the argument **needs** to be true. Test a choice by **negating** it: if the negation destroys the argument, it is the assumption.

**Why it matters.** The negation test cleanly separates required assumptions from merely helpful or out-of-scope statements.

**Example.** "Lowering lead in water will cut lead poisoning." Assumption: residents get meaningful lead exposure *from water*. Negate it — they get none from water — and the conclusion collapses, confirming it was assumed.

title: RC: read for structure, not detail
body:
**Rule.** On the first read, map the **structure**: main point, the author's opinion, and the role each paragraph plays. Don't memorize details — you can return for them.

**Why it matters.** Main-idea and function questions reward a structural read; detail questions are open-book, so locate and verify rather than recall.

**Example.** A passage that states a classic model, then spends three paragraphs on a scholar who *qualified* it, has the purpose "describe research that complicated an accepted view" — recognizable from structure alone.

title: The Data Sufficiency framework (AD / BCE)
body:
**Rule.** Decide *whether* you can answer, not the answer itself. Test statement (1) alone: if sufficient, the answer is **A or D**; if not, **B, C, or E**. Then test (2) alone, and only combine them if neither alone works.

**Why it matters.** The AD/BCE split prevents the cardinal DS sin — letting one statement's information leak into your judgment of the other.

**Example.** "Is $x>0$? (1) $x^2>0$ (2) $x^3>0$." (1) allows negatives → not sufficient (eliminate A, D). (2) forces $x>0$ → sufficient → answer **B**.

title: Reading tables & graphs under time pressure
body:
**Rule.** Read the question **before** the data, then sort or scan only for what's asked. Watch units, axis scales, and totals-vs-rates. Each statement in Table Analysis is independent — judge it on its own.

**Why it matters.** These items reward targeted lookups, not full comprehension. Sorting by the relevant column answers most "highest/lowest/how many" prompts instantly.

**Example.** "Does the highest total also have the highest per-capita value?" Sort by total, note the top row, then check its per-capita rank — often they differ, making the statement false.

title: Two-Part & Multi-Source approach
body:
**Rule.** In **Two-Part Analysis**, the two columns are usually linked by one relationship; solve each column's condition separately against the shared answer list. In **Multi-Source Reasoning**, the answer often lives at the **intersection** of two tabs — check every condition against *all* sources.

**Why it matters.** Both formats punish using only one source or one column's logic. Cross-checking is the whole game.

**Example.** Revenue $R = 3u + 12$. "Units when $R=48$" gives $u=12$; "Revenue when $u=20$" gives $R=72$ — two independent reads of one equation, one per column.
